title: Gradient Boosting: Step-by-Step Learning
concept: Gradient Boosting builds a sequence of trees where each new tree is trained to correct the mistakes made by the previous ones, slowly minimizing overall error.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How do Random Forests build their trees?

options.text: They train many trees independently in parallel, and then average their votes.
They train one giant tree and prune it until only one leaf is left.
They train trees sequentially, with each tree rewriting the rules of the previous one.

exercises.explain: Correct! Random Forests build independent trees in parallel. But today we will explore how trees can work step-by-step to fix each other's mistakes!

cards.title: Adjusting Your Aim
cards.body: Imagine practicing basketball shots. Your first shot goes too far to the left. For your next shot, you don't start from scratch with a random throw — you adjust your aim to the right to correct your previous mistake. You repeat this until you hit the center of the hoop.

cards.title: What is Gradient Boosting?
cards.body: Gradient Boosting is an ensemble method that trains decision trees one after another. Instead of looking at the original target, each new tree is trained specifically to predict and correct the errors (or loss) left behind by the previous trees.

cards.title: A Chain of Book Editors
cards.body: Imagine writing a book. The first writer's draft is full of typos. Instead of rewriting the whole book, the second writer only notes down the corrections. The third writer looks at the second's corrected draft and only fixes the remaining grammar issues. Together, they create a perfect book.

cards.title: Predicting Toy Prices
cards.body: Suppose we want to predict a toy's price ($10). Tree 1 predicts $8. The error is +$2. Instead of building Tree 2 to predict the original $10, we train Tree 2 to predict the +$2 error. By adding Tree 1 and Tree 2's predictions, we get $8 + $2 = $10!

cards.title: Step-by-Step, Not All at Once
cards.body: You might think all ensemble models train trees at the same time. In fact, Gradient Boosting must train trees sequentially. Because a new tree needs to know the mistakes of the previous trees, it cannot start until the ones before it are fully finished.

cards.title: Parallel vs. Sequential
cards.body: How tree training flows differ in ensemble methods:

left.text:
Trains trees in parallel.
Trees are independent.
Votes are averaged at the end.

right.text:
Trains trees sequentially.
Each tree corrects the previous ones.
Predictions are added up step-by-step.

exercises.prompt: In Gradient Boosting, what is the main goal of each new decision tree?

options.text: To correct the errors made by all the previous trees combined.
To vote independently on the final category label.
To find the average value of the original dataset.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Each new tree in gradient boosting is trained specifically on the errors (or loss) of the previous trees to correct them.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Gradient Boosting trains all of its decision trees at the same time in parallel.
exercises.explain: False. Gradient Boosting trains trees one after another (sequentially), because each tree needs to know the mistakes of the previous ones.

exercises.sentence: While Random Forests train trees in parallel, Gradient Boosting trains them ___.
exercises.explain: Gradient Boosting is a sequential process where trees are built step-by-step to correct prior mistakes.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes a Gradient Boosting approach to learning?

options.text: A student takes a practice test, checks their incorrect answers, and studies only those topics for the next test.
A class of 100 students all take the same exam at the same time, and we average their scores.
A teacher selects a single student and gives them the same test over and over without showing them what they got wrong.

exercises.explain: Studying only the incorrect answers is just like gradient boosting: training the next step on the mistakes of the previous one!

exercises.prompt: You are building a model to predict water usage in a school. The first tree makes a rough prediction, but leaves some patterns unexplained (errors). How does Gradient Boosting handle this?

options.text: It trains a second tree to predict the leftover errors of the first tree, and adds their predictions together.
It throws away the first tree and starts a new one from scratch.
It averages the first tree's prediction with a random number to hide the errors.
It builds 100 independent trees at the same time and averages their predictions.

exercises.explain: Perfect! In Gradient Boosting, we train each subsequent tree to predict the errors (residuals) of the previous step. We add their predictions together to make a highly accurate final guess!
